text.title: Load Balancing Concepts Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the LB decision. Each pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Choose L7 when the routing decision depends on HTTP semantics (path, header, cookie, SSL termination at LB, API gateway features). Choose L4 when routing is protocol-agnostic and latency/throughput is the dominant constraint.

rationale.source: AWS ELB docs, HAProxy architecture guide

rule.rationale: L7 inspects the application payload — costs CPU but unlocks routing, header manipulation, WAF. L4 only sees IP + port — far cheaper but blind to content. Picking L7 for a pure TCP forwarder wastes 2-4x CPU; picking L4 for an HTTP API blocks every header-based feature.

rule.statement: Use a static algorithm (round-robin, IP-hash) only when backends have identical capacity AND request cost is uniform. Use a dynamic algorithm (least-connections, least-response-time) when backends are heterogeneous OR connection longevity varies OR request cost is bimodal.

rationale.source: NGINX upstream module docs, F5 BIG-IP best-practices

rule.rationale: Static algorithms have no runtime overhead but cannot react to actual load. Dynamic ones track per-backend state and route to the least-loaded instance. Mixing a static algorithm with long-lived connections creates queue imbalance that the LB cannot self-correct.

rule.statement: Configure health checks as HTTP GET against a dedicated `/health` endpoint (NOT a TCP-port-open check) with interval 10-30 s, timeout 5 s, healthy-threshold 2-3, unhealthy-threshold 2-3.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook ch. 10, AWS ELB best practices

rule.rationale: TCP-open only proves the kernel is alive — the app process may be deadlocked. An HTTP `/health` endpoint that touches the same dependencies the real request would touch (DB ping, cache ping) is the only signal that maps to user impact.

rule.statement: Externalise session state to a shared store (Redis, DynamoDB) by default. Use sticky sessions ONLY when (a) the app cannot be refactored AND (b) loss of user session on backend failure is acceptable AND (c) a documented fallback exists.
rule.rationale: Sticky sessions make backends non-interchangeable, breaking even distribution and turning every backend death into a session-loss event. Externalised state makes auto-scaling, blue/green deploys, and canary releases possible without user-visible disruption.

rule.statement: The LB tier itself MUST be redundant (active-active or active-passive pair, multi-AZ minimum). A single LB instance is a single point of failure that defeats the purpose of fronting the service.

rationale.source: AWS Well-Architected Reliability pillar, Cloudflare LB docs

rule.rationale: If the LB dies, every backend behind it becomes unreachable regardless of health. Active-passive with sub-30 s failover is the minimum acceptable; active-active across AZs is the production default.

rule.statement: Before signing off the LB design, execute a deliberate failover drill: kill one backend (verify drain + reroute), kill the active LB (verify peer takeover), and load-test at 2x expected peak to confirm headroom.
rule.rationale: Untested HA is design-time HA. Production failover always finds the assumption no-one wrote down. The drill catches mis-set drain timeouts, incorrect health-check intervals, and DNS-TTL surprises in GSLB topology.

rule.statement: If the Applies If preconditions in AGENTS.md do not all hold, OR any Skip If trigger fires, the agent MUST skip this methodology and pick a different one — applying the rules here on a mismatched input produces wrong output.
rule.rationale: Methodology selection errors cascade. The cheapest place to abort is before any rule fires.
